items: Never place a tent in a dry wash or drainage channel; storms upstream can send water through it.
Before pitching a tent, look up for dead limbs, leaning snags, loose rock, and other overhead hazards.
Use an established durable campsite when available instead of clearing a new patch of vegetation.
Avoid the lowest point in a campsite; cold air and rainwater both settle downhill.
Stake and tension the shelter before weather arrives, not while wind is already trying to take it.
Ventilate a tent even in cool weather; trapped breath can soak insulation with condensation.
A sleeping pad does more than add comfort—it slows heat loss into the ground.
Put your headlamp where you can reach it before sunset; darkness should not begin with a gear search.
Low voices and dim lights at night protect other campers’ rest and make wildlife easier to hear.
Before leaving camp, walk a slow grid for trash, cord, stakes, food scraps, and forgotten gear.
Use camp stoves outside and away from tents; open doors and windows do not make indoor stove use safe.
Never burn charcoal inside a tent, cabin, vehicle, or other enclosed shelter.
Carbon monoxide has no color or smell, so you cannot rely on your senses to detect it.
Headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, chest pain, or confusion can signal carbon-monoxide poisoning.
Set a stove on a level, stable, nonflammable surface where nobody can kick or trip over it.
Connect and inspect stove fuel before ignition; follow the manufacturer’s lighting order exactly.
Turn pot handles away from traffic so a passing knee or sleeve cannot dump boiling water.
Never leave a burning stove unattended, even for a quick trip to the tent.
Let the stove cool before changing or disconnecting fuel canisters.
Keep the cooking area organized and separate from sleeping spaces, play areas, and crowded paths.
If you can hear thunder, you are close enough to be struck by lightning.
During lightning, the safest nearby shelter is a substantial building or hard-topped enclosed vehicle.
A lone tree is not safe lightning shelter; avoid ridges, summits, open fields, and isolated tall objects.
Wait at least 30 minutes after the last thunder before resuming outdoor activity.
Flash flooding can arrive from a storm you cannot see or hear because the rain fell upstream.
Never enter flowing floodwater on foot or in a vehicle; depth and road damage are hard to judge.
If water begins rising in a canyon or wash, move to higher ground immediately rather than gathering gear.
Monsoon outflow winds can arrive before rain; secure shelters and loose gear when clouds begin building.
A summer mountain storm can drop the temperature quickly; rain protection and insulation still matter in Arizona.
In monsoon season, an early start can reduce time spent on exposed high ground during afternoon storms.
Heat, altitude, pack weight, and poor sleep all slow people down; adjust the plan instead of pushing the schedule.
Buddy checks catch sunburn, confusion, unusual fatigue, missing water, and gear problems before they become emergencies.
Headache, nausea, dizziness, or unusual fatigue at altitude deserve attention and should not be hidden.
Clouds do not block all ultraviolet radiation; sunscreen, hats, and coverage still matter.
Build regular water breaks into the group rhythm instead of waiting for someone to admit they are thirsty.
On long hot days, water and normal salty food usually work together better than drinking water alone without eating.
Clean hands before cooking or eating; stomach illness spreads easily through a camp kitchen.
Wash and dry shared utensils promptly instead of letting food residue sit overnight.
Dispose of toothpaste and wash water according to local rules; “biodegradable” does not mean harmless anywhere.
Do not share water bottles, lip balm, toothbrushes, or eating utensils.
Rest is a safety tool: tired hikers make worse navigation, cooking, and judgment decisions.
Report pain, dizziness, nausea, breathing trouble, or equipment failure early—before the group is far from help.
Start slower than feels necessary; a sustainable pace usually beats repeated bursts and long recovery stops.
Shorter steps on steep climbs reduce strain and help maintain an even rhythm.
Descend with controlled steps rather than running; tired legs and loose rock are a bad combination.
Give extra spacing when using trekking poles so tips do not catch the hiker behind you.
Step over logs when possible instead of onto them; bark can roll or become slick.
On easy scrambles, keep three points of contact and test holds before trusting full body weight.
Regroup at every junction, water source, difficult crossing, and major change in terrain.
Before passing, call out politely and wait until the trail is wide enough for everyone to stay on durable ground.
Store food and scented items away from sleeping areas according to local wildlife rules.
Never feed wildlife, even once; animals quickly learn to associate camps and people with food.
Treat trash like food: seal it, store it securely, and pack every piece out.
Look before placing hands or feet into cracks, under rocks, or beneath stored gear.
If a snake is near camp, keep people back and give it a clear escape route; do not surround or move it.
If an animal changes direction, posture, or behavior because of you, you are too close.
Keeping food and scented items out of tents reduces unwanted nighttime visitors.
Where pets are allowed, keep them controlled; they can chase wildlife, attract predators, or disturb other campers.
